text: This video demonstrates a transvaginal approach to the repair of recurrent pouch vaginal fistula.
The patient had previously undergone a vaginal plug procedure and a Martius flap.
A seton is visibly in place at the beginning of the anal surgery procedure.
The surgeon places a lone star retractor to expose the surgical field and infiltrates the vaginal wall with an adrenaline solution, likely to reduce bleeding and enhance visibility.
The surgeon removes the ceton and inserts a fistula probe into the fistula track, followed by the injection of methylene blue dye to visualize the track.
At the start of the operation, the surgeon places multiple stay sutures to expose the fistula track, preparing the area for further dissection.
Three stay sutures are placed above the fistula track, with the suture material hooked around the lone star retractor to enhance exposure of the surgical area.
The surgeon places the third suture, carefully attaching it to the fistula probe and hooking it around the lone star retractor to maintain exposure of the fistula track.
Three stay sutures are placed inferior to the vaginal opening of the fistula tract, with the sutures hooked around the lone star retractor to aid in exposure.
The surgeon further exposes the fistula track using stay sutures and retractors, preparing for dissection and removal of the track.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully dissect around the vaginal opening of the fistula, with the fistula probe remaining in position for guidance.
The surgeon manipulates the fistula track, enhancing its exposure and facilitating easier dissection, with the aid of the stay sutures hooked around the lone star retractor.
Possibly because of the previous vaginal plug, the collagen plug, there's been some reaction around the track which facilitates the dissection down to the pouch itself.

A clip is applied to the fistula track, aiding in the dissection process. The surgeon pulls on the track to facilitate sharp dissection down to the pouch opening of the fistula, revealing the short length of the fistula track.
The sureness of the track probably explains the poor results that we and others have had using collagen plugs in this situation.
The surgeon uses a suction device to define the edges of the fistula openings, preparing to close the internal opening.
The surgeon uses dissolvable monofilament suture material to close the pouch side of the fistula track.
A finger is placed in the rectum to expose and define the internal opening on the pouch side of the fistula.
As is often the case with such fistulas, the level of the fistula is at the site of the previous staple anastomosis.
and may reflect erosion of the staples through the adjacent tissues to create these parent vaginal fistulas.
The surgeon defines the tissues on the vaginal side of the fistula, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon encounters difficulty dissecting tissues on the left side where a previous Martius flap had been positioned, indicating dense tissue adhesion in this area.
The surgeon identifies remaining tissues of the Martius flap, freeing a section to cover the internal opening of the fistula.
On the right side of the vagina, the surgeon observes more pliable tissues, contrasting with the left side where a Martius flap had previously been raised.
The surgeon dissects the vaginal musculature and attached levators, freeing them to provide adequate muscle tissue for burying and closing the internal opening of the fistula tract.
The surgeon elevates the vaginal mucosa using local anesthetic to facilitate further dissection and exposure of the underlying tissues.
The surgical field shows relatively bloody tissues, which is a common occurrence during this stage of the procedure.
It's important to have good suction, good exposure and good lighting to aid dissection.
Minimising tension by full mobilisation of the vaginal musculature should help improve the chance of success.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a cephalic direction, carefully separating tissue layers to expose the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to free up the tissue, ensuring that the interlobe of the fistula is fully closed by the vaginal musculature.
The surgeon places a suture at the top end of the dissection site, aiming to bring muscle tissue across to close the upper aspect of the fistula where dissection has occurred.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the left Martius flap, carefully separating the tissue to aid in the closure of the fistula.
The yellow sutures are visible in the center of the image, used to close over the internal defect and further secure the internal opening of the fistula.
The surgeon examines the vaginal musculature, preparing to insert sutures to approximate the two sides of the muscle tissue.
The surgeon positions well-nourished, well-vascularized muscle tissue over the previous site of the tract to ensure closure.
The surgeon uses a monofilament suture to minimize the risk of infection during the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a pyretractor to expose the vault of the vagina, ensuring that the vaginal mucosa is closed along its entire length.
The surgeon buries the muscular repair by covering it with surrounding tissue, ensuring the repair is not exposed.
Coamoxiclav was given at the start of the procedure.
The patient was given five days post-op.
The surgeon completes the dissection after closing the vaginal mucosa with a monofilament suture, ensuring the closure is secure along its length.
The surgeon completes the operation by inserting a urinary catheter and placing a vaginal pack soaked in estrogen cream.
